mechanism: Proposed mechanism: HCA has been studied for effects on appetite and body weight; specific mechanisms in humans are not established clearly enough to treat as confirmed.
safetyNotes: Potential unsafe use due to reported liver damage cases, including severe cases; gastrointestinal adverse effects such as nausea and diarrhea; limited pregnancy/lactation data.
description: A tree native to India / South Asia; supplement preparations commonly emphasize the fruit rind, which contains hydroxycitric acid (HCA). Historically used as a food preservative and in folk medicine.
dosage: No direct dosage data. Contextual inference: Appetite suppressant; metabolism enhancer. Herbs often act via neurotransmitter modulation; antioxidant effects; or enzyme inhibition.. interactions: Concerns with liver-affecting drugs and serotonergic drugs; medication review advised.
legalStatus: Legal but subject to regulatory review
preparation: Commonly sold as extracts of the fruit rind and multi-ingredient weight-loss products containing garcinia extract/HCA.
summary: Garcinia (hydroxycitric-acid-containing fruit rind extract) is marketed for appetite control and weight loss, but human evidence for meaningful weight-loss benefit is inconsistent; there are multiple reports of clinically apparent liver injury associated with products labeled as containing garcinia, including severe cases.
